name: Taowu
description: Taowu, also known as Hundun, is one of the Four Perils (四凶) in Chinese mythology. Described as a monstrous, chaotic being, Taowu embodies ignorance and stubbornness. Legends often depict it as a large, fearsome creature with a mix of animalistic features, symbolizing the untamed and malevolent aspects of human nature. Taowu is associated with hindering good and promoting evil, representing the dangers of unchecked chaos and the importance of maintaining order. It is said to possess immense strength and a relentless drive to cause destruction, making it a formidable adversary in mythological tales. The name itself implies a lack of understanding and a refusal to learn or adapt, highlighting the creature's inherent negativity. Taowu serves as a cautionary figure, reminding people of the consequences of ignorance and the need for wisdom and discernment. Stories involving Taowu often emphasize the importance of overcoming inner demons and resisting the allure of chaos.
subtitle: Embodiment of Ignorance and Chaos
appearance: Taowu is often depicted as a large, monstrous creature with a mix of animal features. Descriptions vary, but it is commonly portrayed as having a bovine body, a human face, tiger-like paws, and a long tail. Some accounts describe it as resembling a bear or a monstrous dog. Its fur is often described as being dark and coarse, and its eyes are said to glow with malevolent intent. The overall impression is one of immense power and untamed ferocity.
behavior: Taowu is characterized by its chaotic and destructive behavior. It actively seeks to disrupt order and promote evil. It is stubborn and refuses to listen to reason or guidance. Taowu is driven by a desire to cause harm and revels in the suffering of others. It is a creature of pure malice, representing the darkest aspects of human nature.

keyMyths.title: One of the Four Perils
keyMyths.description: Taowu is one of the Four Perils, monstrous creatures banished by the legendary emperors. It represents chaos and ignorance, a constant threat to the established order.

sources.author: Sima Qian
sources.description: An ancient Chinese historical text that mentions Taowu as one of the Four Perils.
sources.text: An ancient Chinese historical text that mentions Taowu as one of the Four Perils.

sources.author: Various
sources.description: Although not explicitly named as Taowu, similar monstrous creatures embodying chaos and destruction are described.
